schools.summary: Gill: God said unto Jacob when he was in great distress, on account of the slaughter of the Shechemites by his sons, not knowing what step to take or course to steer for the safety of him and his family; then God, for his comfort and direction, bade him go to Bethel; the witnesses note God commanding Jacob to arise, go up to Bethel, dwell there, and make an altar to the God who appeared to him when he fled from Esau — the timely call to Bethel, recalling Jacob to his vow and to worship after the disorder at Shechem (Jacob in distress and danger after his sons' bloody deed, God breaking in with a gracious command: 'Arise, go up to Bethel, and dwell there: and make there an altar unto God, that appeared unto thee when thou fleddest from the face of Esau thy brother'; the mercy and meaning of the call, that God, finding Jacob fearful and at a loss, directs him to 'go up to Bethel,' the very place where God had met him twenty years before in the dream of the ladder when he fled from Esau, and where Jacob had vowed to make it God's house; the gentle reproof and reminder in it, that Jacob, who had lingered at Shechem and let trouble come upon his house, is called back to Bethel to pay his vow and worship; that God reminds him of the appearance 'when thou fleddest from... Esau,' that the God of Bethel is faithful still, so God calls Jacob to Bethel, to his vow and his altar).

themes: Arise, go up to Bethel, and dwell there
make there an altar unto God, that appeared unto thee when thou fleddest from... Esau
God's timely call to Bethel after the disorder at Shechem
recalling Jacob to his vow and to the faithful God of Bethel

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob said to his household and all with him, Put away the strange gods that are among you, and be clean, and change your garments; Gill: this was said to his wives and children and all his company; the witnesses note Jacob bidding his household put away the strange gods, be clean, and change their garments — the reformation of the household, a putting away of idols and a cleansing before the journey to God's house (Jacob, called to Bethel, first setting his own house in order: 'Put away the strange gods that are among you, and be clean, and change your garments'; the threefold charge, first 'put away the strange gods,' the idols among them, whether the teraphim Rachel had taken, the images and earrings of the spoil of Shechem, or any other heathen objects, that no idol go up with them to God's house, second 'be clean,' wash and purify their bodies, a symbol of inward cleansing from sin, and third 'change your garments,' put on fresh clean clothing, a token of renewed and reverent worship; that all three were outward signs of an inward reformation, that they might come to Bethel cleansed of idolatry and prepared to worship the true God; Jacob's care, as the head of his house, to lead them in this reformation, so Jacob purges his household of idols and prepares them for Bethel).

themes: Put away the strange gods that are among you
and be clean, and change your garments
the reformation of the household before Bethel
outward signs of inward cleansing from idolatry

schools.summary: Gill: thus prepared and purged, their tents clear of idols, their bodies washed with pure water, and their garments new, neat, and clean, all symbolical of inward purity and of freedom from idolatry, they should arise and go up to Bethel; the witnesses note Jacob calling them to go up to Bethel, to build an altar to the God who answered him in distress and was with him in the way — the grateful purpose of worship, owning the God who had heard and kept him through all his journey (Jacob declaring the reason and goal of the going-up: 'let us arise, and go up to Bethel; and I will make there an altar unto God, who answered me in the day of my distress, and was with me in the way which I went'; the grateful confession in his words, that Jacob would build an altar to 'God, who answered me in the day of my distress,' the God who heard his cry when he fled from Esau and in all his troubles, 'and was with me in the way which I went,' the God whose presence had kept and prospered him through the whole long journey, there and back; that the altar was to be a thank-offering, a public owning of God's faithfulness in answering and accompanying him; that Jacob, now purged and grateful, leads his cleansed household up to worship, so Jacob purposes an altar of thanksgiving to his faithful God at Bethel).

themes: let us arise, and go up to Bethel
who answered me in the day of my distress, and was with me in the way which I went
the grateful purpose of worship at Bethel
a thank-offering owning God's faithfulness through all his journey

schools.summary: Gill: they gave unto Jacob all the strange gods which were in their hands, whether in the hands of his servants, or of the captives taken at Shechem, or in the hands of his sons who had them along with the spoil they took there, and all their earrings, worn as amulets or superstitious charms; Jacob hid them under the oak by Shechem; the witnesses note the household giving Jacob all the strange gods and earrings, which he hid under the oak by Shechem — the ready surrender of the idols, the household obeying and the tokens of idolatry buried (the reformation carried out at once: 'they gave unto Jacob all the strange gods which were in their hand, and all their earrings which were in their ears; and Jacob hid them under the oak which was by Shechem'; the obedience of the household, that they 'gave unto Jacob all the strange gods,' delivering up every idol from whatever source, servants, captives, the spoil of Shechem, 'and all their earrings,' the earrings worn as amulets or superstitious charms or fashioned with idolatrous figures; Jacob's dealing with them, that he 'hid them under the oak which was by Shechem,' burying these unclean things out of sight that they might be wholly put away and never used again; that the household's ready compliance shows the power of Jacob's godly leadership and the genuineness of the reformation, so the idols and superstitious ornaments are surrendered and buried).

themes: they gave unto Jacob all the strange gods... and all their earrings
and Jacob hid them under the oak which was by Shechem
the ready surrender and burial of the idols
the genuineness of the reformation shown in their obedience

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the terror of God, a supernatural awe sent by the Almighty, was upon the cities that were round about, so that they were afraid to attack Jacob; Gill: they journeyed from Shechem to Bethel; the witnesses note Jacob and his family journeying, the terror of God upon the surrounding cities so they did not pursue — the divine protection that answered Jacob's fear, God himself guarding the cleansed household on the way (the very danger Jacob had dreaded removed by God's hand: 'they journeyed: and the terror of God was upon the cities that were round about them, and they did not pursue after the sons of Jacob'; the mercy and wonder of it, that Jacob had feared the Canaanites and Perizzites would 'gather together against' him to avenge Shechem, but as the family 'journeyed' God sent 'the terror of God,' a supernatural dread and awe as Clarke notes, upon the cities round about, so that, though they had cause and power to fall on Jacob, 'they did not pursue'; that God thus answered Jacob's fear and kept his promise to be with him, restraining the heathen and shielding the now-cleansed household on their way to Bethel; the connection, that the household, having put away their idols, was now under God's special protection, so God's terror guards Jacob and none dare pursue).

themes: they journeyed: and the terror of God was upon the cities that were round about them
and they did not pursue after the sons of Jacob
the divine protection guarding the journey
the danger Jacob dreaded removed by God's hand

schools.summary: Gill: the place Jacob had called Bethel when he was there before was formerly called Luz, and is here said to be in the land of Canaan, that is, in that part of it; the witnesses note Jacob coming to Luz, which is Bethel, in Canaan, with all his people — the safe arrival at the place of the vow, the whole company brought to Bethel (Jacob reaching the appointed place: 'Jacob came to Luz, which is in the land of Canaan, that is, Bethel, he and all the people that were with him'; the naming of the place, that 'Luz' was its old common name but Jacob had called it 'Bethel,' the house of God, when God first met him there, the Scripture joining both names, 'Luz... that is, Bethel,' and marking it 'in the land of Canaan,' the land of promise; the completeness of the arrival, 'he and all the people that were with him,' that Jacob came not alone but brought his whole household and company safely to Bethel, God having shielded them all the way; that he was now at the very spot where he had vowed to worship, ready to pay his vow, so Jacob and all his people come safely to Bethel).

themes: So Jacob came to Luz, which is in the land of Canaan, that is, Bethel
he and all the people that were with him
the safe arrival at the place of the vow
brought whole to Bethel, God having shielded the company

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'El-bethel' means 'the God of the house of God' (the strong God, the house of the strong God); Gill: he built there an altar, as he was bid to do and as he had promised; the witnesses note Jacob building the altar and calling the place El-bethel, because there God appeared to him when he fled from Esau — the vow at last paid, the altar built and named for the God who had met him there (Jacob fulfilling the command and his own long vow: 'he built there an altar, and called the place Elbethel: because there God appeared unto him, when he fled from the face of his brother'; the obedience and gratitude, that Jacob 'built there an altar,' as God had bidden and as he himself had vowed twenty years before, paying at last the vow he had made when he fled from Esau, and he 'called the place Elbethel,' the God of Bethel or the God of the house of God; the deepening of the name, that when he first came he called the place 'Bethel,' the house of God, but now he names it 'El-bethel,' the God of the house of God, looking past the place to the God who there appeared; the reason given, 'because there God appeared unto him, when he fled,' keeping the memory of that first great meeting, so Jacob pays his vow, building and naming the altar for the God of Bethel).

themes: he built there an altar, and called the place Elbethel
because there God appeared unto him, when he fled from the face of his brother
the vow paid, the altar named for the God of Bethel
looking past the place to the God who there appeared

schools.summary: Gill: Deborah, a nurse of Rebekah's, came with her to Canaan when she married Isaac, and is generally thought to be this Deborah, now dying quickly after they were come to Bethel; she was buried beneath Bethel under an oak, the name of which was called Allon-bachuth, the oak of weeping; the witnesses note Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, dying and being buried beneath Bethel under an oak named Allon-bachuth — the death of the aged nurse and the oak of weeping that marked the grief (at Bethel a death befalling the household: 'Deborah Rebekah's nurse died, and she was buried beneath Bethel under an oak: and the name of it was called Allonbachuth'; who Deborah was, the nurse of Rebekah, Jacob's mother, who had come with Rebekah from Padan-aram long before when she married Isaac and was now, in great age, part of Jacob's household, her death at Bethel; the burial 'beneath Bethel under an oak,' and the name given the place, 'Allonbachuth,' the oak of weeping, a token of the grief and mourning that her death stirred; the tenderness in the record, that a faithful old servant's death was felt and wept, her grave marked and named; that this death was the first of several sorrows that would soon come upon Jacob, so the aged Deborah dies and is mourned at the oak of weeping).

themes: Deborah Rebekah's nurse died, and she was buried beneath Bethel under an oak
and the name of it was called Allonbachuth
the death of Deborah, buried under the oak of weeping
a faithful old servant's death felt and wept

schools.summary: Gill: God appeared unto Jacob again at Bethel, as he had at Shechem when he bid him go thither, or rather as he had at the brook Jabbok where he said to him the same things as here; the witnesses note God appearing to Jacob again, after his coming out of Padan-aram, and blessing him — the renewed and gracious appearance, God meeting Jacob once more at the place of his vow to bless him (the altar built, God coming to Jacob with a fresh manifestation: 'God appeared unto Jacob again, when he came out of Padanaram, and blessed him'; the word 'again,' marking this as a renewed appearance, that God had met Jacob at this very place twenty years before in the ladder-dream and now, 'when he came out of Padanaram' and back to Bethel, God 'appeared unto Jacob again,' confirming his favour; the grace of it, that God did not leave Jacob to worship alone but came to meet him, crowning his obedience and his altar with a personal appearance and a blessing; that 'blessed him' sums the whole, God bestowing on Jacob fresh assurance of his covenant favour as the verses following unfold, so God appears again to Jacob at Bethel and blesses him).

themes: God appeared unto Jacob again, when he came out of Padanaram
and blessed him
God's renewed appearance and blessing at Bethel
meeting Jacob once more at the place of his vow

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: God said, Thy name is Jacob; thy name shall not be called any more Jacob, but Israel shall be thy name; Gill: Jacob was the name his parents gave him at his birth, by which he had always been called; the witnesses note God telling Jacob his name should no more be Jacob but Israel, and calling his name Israel — the solemn confirming of the Peniel name-change, sealing afresh the honour of Israel (God renewing and sealing the new name: 'Thy name is Jacob: thy name shall not be called any more Jacob, but Israel shall be thy name: and he called his name Israel'; that this is a confirming of what was given at Peniel, where the wrestling Angel had named him Israel, and now, at Bethel, God himself solemnly ratifies it, that the name might be established and sure; the meaning of the change, from 'Jacob,' the supplanter, his birth-name, to 'Israel,' a prince with God, who had power with God and prevailed, the new name marking his changed standing and the honour God put upon him; the doubling of the act, the name given at Peniel and confirmed at Bethel, setting it beyond doubt, the honour twice sealed, so God confirms Jacob's new name Israel at Bethel).

themes: thy name shall not be called any more Jacob, but Israel shall be thy name
and he called his name Israel
the confirming of the Peniel name-change at Bethel
the honour twice sealed, marking him a prince with God

schools.summary: Gill: God, as God Almighty (El-Shaddai), was able to protect and defend him, to fulfil all the promises made to him, and to supply him with everything he wanted; the witnesses note God declaring himself God Almighty, bidding Jacob be fruitful, and promising a nation, a company of nations, and kings from his loins — the great covenant-promise renewed, the title El-Shaddai sealing it as sure (God grounding the blessing on his almighty name and a vast promise: 'I am God Almighty: be fruitful and multiply; a nation and a company of nations shall be of thee, and kings shall come out of thy loins'; the weight of the name 'God Almighty,' El-Shaddai, the same name by which God had appeared to Abraham, the all-sufficient God able to protect, to fulfil, and to supply, so that the promise rests on omnipotence; the greatness of the promise, 'be fruitful and multiply,' that from Jacob should come 'a nation,' Israel, 'and a company of nations,' the tribes and the peoples gathered to them, and most gloriously 'kings shall come out of thy loins,' the royal line, the kings of Israel and Judah and at last the King Messiah, Christ, of the seed of Jacob; that this renews to Jacob the very promise made to Abraham and Isaac, so God Almighty renews the promise of a nation, nations, and kings).

debates.question: What is the fullest scope of 'kings shall come out of thy loins'?

sides.position: The royal line of Israel and Judah, crowned at last in the King Messiah, Christ, of the seed of Jacob

themes: I am God Almighty: be fruitful and multiply
a nation and a company of nations shall be of thee, and kings shall come out of thy loins
the covenant-promise renewed under the name God Almighty
the royal line crowned at last in the King Messiah

schools.summary: Gill: the land of Canaan, which God had by promise given to his grandfather Abraham and his father Isaac, he would give to Jacob, thus renewing the grant to him and to his seed after him; the witnesses note God promising Jacob and his seed the land he had given Abraham and Isaac — the renewal of the land-promise, the inheritance confirmed to Jacob and his seed (God adding the promise of the land to the promise of the seed: 'the land which I gave Abraham and Isaac, to thee I will give it, and to thy seed after thee will I give the land'; the continuity of the covenant, that the very 'land which I gave Abraham and Isaac,' Canaan promised to the fathers, God now gives anew 'to thee,' to Jacob, 'and to thy seed after thee,' confirming the inheritance to the third generation and to the line that should follow; that the land, though Jacob held but a parcel by purchase and dwelt as a pilgrim, was sure to his seed by God's word, who would in time give them the whole; that the two promises together, a numerous royal seed and the land of Canaan, are the heart of the Abrahamic covenant now sealed afresh to Jacob, so God renews to Jacob the promise of the land for him and his seed).

themes: the land which I gave Abraham and Isaac, to thee I will give it
and to thy seed after thee will I give the land
the renewal of the land-promise to Jacob and his seed
the inheritance sure by God's word, though Jacob dwelt a pilgrim

schools.summary: Gill: by this it seems that there was a visible appearance of the glory of the Lord, as Onkelos, or of the Shechinah, as Jonathan, even of the Son of God in a human form, who went up from him; the witnesses note God going up from Jacob in the place where he had talked with him — the close of the divine appearance, God visibly ascending from the place of the meeting (the appearance ending as it came: 'God went up from him in the place where he talked with him'; the visible glory in the phrase, that 'God went up from him' implies a real and visible manifestation of the divine presence, the glory of the LORD or the Shechinah, or as Gill holds the Son of God in human form, which had come down to talk with Jacob and now 'went up from him,' ascending out of his sight; the marking of the place, 'where he talked with him,' the very spot of the meeting made sacred and memorable by God's presence and speech there; that, the covenant renewed and the blessing given, the visible appearance is withdrawn, the divine Visitor ascending, so God ascends from the place where he had spoken with Jacob).

themes: God went up from him
in the place where he talked with him
the close of the visible divine appearance
a real manifestation of the glory, withdrawn after the blessing

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob had set up a pillar in this place before he went to Padan-aram, and some think this pillar is here referred to, renewed and consecrated afresh; he poured a drink offering of wine thereon and poured oil thereon, consecrating it; the witnesses note Jacob setting up a stone pillar in the place of the meeting, pouring on it a drink offering and oil — the memorial-pillar consecrated with offering and oil to mark the sacred place (Jacob marking the spot of God's appearing with a consecrated monument: 'Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he talked with him, even a pillar of stone: and he poured a drink offering thereon, and he poured oil thereon'; the act, that Jacob 'set up a pillar... of stone,' whether a fresh one or the renewing of the one he had raised here twenty years before, to be a standing memorial of God's presence and speech, and he 'poured a drink offering thereon,' a libation of wine, an act of worship and thanksgiving, 'and he poured oil thereon,' anointing and consecrating the pillar, setting it apart as sacred; the deepening of his earlier act, that at first he had poured only oil on the stone but now, richer in grace and gratitude, he adds the drink offering, a fuller worship; that the pillar, consecrated with oil and offering, stood as a lasting witness of the covenant renewed, so Jacob consecrates a memorial-pillar with offering and oil).

themes: Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he talked with him, even a pillar of stone
and he poured a drink offering thereon, and he poured oil thereon
the consecrated memorial-pillar at Bethel
a fuller worship marking the place of God's appearing

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob confirmed the name he had before given it when he went to Mesopotamia, and now upon his return renews and establishes it; the witnesses note Jacob calling the place where God spoke with him Bethel — the renewing of the name 'house of God,' sealing the sacred place by the renewed appearance (Jacob confirming the old name afresh: 'Jacob called the name of the place where God spake with him, Bethel'; that this was a renewing, not a first naming, that Jacob had called the place 'Bethel,' the house of God, twenty years before when God first met him there in the dream, and now, upon his return and after this fresh appearance, he renews and establishes the name, sealing it by the new meeting; the fitness of the name 'Bethel,' the house of God, the place where God had now twice spoken with him, twice made it the gate of heaven; that the doubled naming, like the doubled name-change, set the matter firm, the place forever marked as God's house, so Jacob renews the name Bethel, the house of God).

themes: Jacob called the name of the place where God spake with him, Bethel
the renewing of the name 'house of God'
sealing the sacred place by the renewed appearance
the place twice marked as God's house

schools.summary: Gill: they removed from Bethel toward Bethlehem, twelve miles from Bethel, on their way to Hebron, and when there was but a little way to Ephrath, Rachel travailed; the witnesses note Jacob and his family journeying from Bethel, and Rachel coming into hard labour near Ephrath — the resuming of the journey and the onset of Rachel's painful travail (leaving Bethel, the family moving on and sorrow meeting them on the way: 'they journeyed from Bethel; and there was but a little way to come to Ephrath: and Rachel travailed, and she had hard labour'; the place and the trial, that when they were 'but a little way' from 'Ephrath,' that is Bethlehem, Rachel 'travailed,' came into childbirth, 'and she had hard labour,' a difficult and dangerous birth; the poignancy of it, that Rachel, the beloved wife who had once cried 'Give me children, or else I die,' now in bearing her second son comes into the very labour that would take her life; that the journey of joy and blessing from Bethel turns, on the road, into a way of sorrow, so near Ephrath Rachel falls into hard and fatal labour).

themes: they journeyed from Bethel; and there was but a little way to come to Ephrath
and Rachel travailed, and she had hard labour
Rachel's hard labour on the road near Ephrath
the beloved wife now in the travail that would take her life

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: when she was in hard labour the midwife said unto her, Fear not, thou shalt have this son also; Gill: it was in the midst of it, and at the worst; the witnesses note the midwife comforting Rachel in her hard labour, telling her not to fear, for she should have this son also — the word of comfort in the extremity, the promise of the longed-for son even as life ebbed (at the worst of the travail, the midwife speaking comfort: 'when she was in hard labour... the midwife said unto her, Fear not; thou shalt have this son also'; the kindness of the word, that the midwife, seeing Rachel's danger and distress, bids her 'Fear not' and gives the comfort dearest to a mother's heart, 'thou shalt have this son also,' another son after Joseph, the very thing Rachel had longed for; the poignancy, that the comfort was a true one, the son indeed born and lived, Benjamin, yet it came at the cost of the mother's life, joy and sorrow meeting in one moment; that Rachel's long desire for a second son was granted even as she died bearing him, so the midwife comforts the dying Rachel with the promise of her son).

themes: the midwife said unto her, Fear not
thou shalt have this son also
the midwife's comfort to Rachel in her extremity
the longed-for son granted even as the mother's life ebbed

schools.summary: Gill: Rachel died in childbirth; she had most passionately desired children, without which she could not live with ease and peace of mind, and now she dies by having one; she called his name Benoni, son of my sorrow, but his father called him Benjamin; the witnesses note Rachel, as her soul departed, naming her son Benoni, but his father calling him Benjamin — the dying mother's sorrowful name turned by the father into a name of blessing (Rachel, in her last breath, naming the child, and Jacob changing it: 'as her soul was in departing, (for she died) that she called his name Benoni: but his father called him Benjamin'; the touching contrast of the two names, that Rachel, dying, called him 'Benoni,' son of my sorrow or son of my pain, naming him from the anguish of her death, but Jacob, his father, would not let the boy bear a name of grief all his days and 'called him Benjamin,' son of the right hand, son of honour, strength, and favour; the tenderness and faith in Jacob's act, turning the mother's name of sorrow into a name of blessing, that the child be remembered not by his mother's death but by his father's love and hope; the poignancy of Rachel's death, the beloved wife dying in the moment of her longed-for joy, so Rachel names her son in sorrow and Jacob in blessing).

debates.question: What is the contrast between the names Benoni and Benjamin?

sides.position: Rachel, dying, named him 'Benoni' (son of my sorrow) from her anguish; Jacob renamed him 'Benjamin' (son of the right hand), turning a name of grief into one of blessing and honour

themes: she called his name Benoni: but his father called him Benjamin
Rachel's dying name of sorrow turned to a name of blessing
'son of my sorrow' made 'son of the right hand'
the child remembered by the father's love, not the mother's death

schools.summary: Gill: Rachel died and was buried in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem, hence called Bethlehem Ephratah; with great pertinency Rachel is represented as if risen from her grave and weeping for her children (Jeremiah 31:15), a word taken up at the slaughter of the innocents (Matthew 2:18); the witnesses note Rachel dying and being buried in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem — the death of the beloved wife and her burial by the road, a grave of lasting memory (the sorrow complete: 'Rachel died, and was buried in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem'; the loss to Jacob, that 'Rachel died,' the wife he had loved above all, for whom he had served fourteen years, taken from him in the midst of the journey; the place of her burial, 'in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem,' by the roadside near the town that would one day be the birthplace of the Messiah; the lasting memory of this grave, that here the prophet pictures 'Rachel weeping for her children,' a word taken up at the slaughter of the innocents at Bethlehem, so that Rachel's grave by Bethlehem became a place of prophetic sorrow, so Rachel dies and is buried by the way to Bethlehem).

themes: And Rachel died
and was buried in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem
the death and burial of the beloved wife near Bethlehem
her grave a place of lasting prophetic sorrow

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob set a sepulchral monument upon her grave in memory of her, which according to Benjamin of Tudela was made of twelve stones after the number of the sons of Jacob; it remained the pillar of Rachel's grave unto this day; the witnesses note Jacob setting a pillar upon Rachel's grave, which remained the pillar of her grave to that day — the loving memorial raised over the beloved wife, a monument that long endured (Jacob honouring Rachel's grave with a standing memorial: 'Jacob set a pillar upon her grave: that is the pillar of Rachel's grave unto this day'; the love and lastingness in it, that Jacob, grieving for his beloved wife, 'set a pillar upon her grave,' a sepulchral monument to mark and honour the place where she lay; that the monument long endured, 'the pillar of Rachel's grave unto this day,' known and pointed out for ages after, so that even in Moses' day and long after the place was remembered; the tenderness of Jacob's grief, that he would not leave her resting-place unmarked but raised a lasting stone over the wife he had loved; that Rachel's tomb remained a known landmark near Bethlehem, so Jacob marks Rachel's grave with a lasting pillar of love).

themes: And Jacob set a pillar upon her grave
that is the pillar of Rachel's grave unto this day
the lasting memorial raised over the beloved wife
a known landmark near Bethlehem, the tenderness of Jacob's grief

schools.summary: Gill: this is the first time Jacob is by Moses called Israel after that name was given him; he stayed near Bethlehem about two months and then removed, spreading his tent beyond the tower of Edar (the tower of the flock); the witnesses note Israel journeying on and spreading his tent beyond the tower of Edar — the continuing of the pilgrim journey, Jacob now called Israel as he moves on from his grief (his mourning for Rachel past, Jacob resuming his way: 'Israel journeyed, and spread his tent beyond the tower of Edar'; the name used, that here 'Israel' is used by Moses for the first time as the patriarch's settled name after the change, the man of God moving on under his honoured name; the place, 'the tower of Edar,' the tower of the flock, a watch-tower of shepherds near Bethlehem, beyond which Israel 'spread his tent,' pitching for a time in the pilgrim manner; that, after the sorrows of Bethel and the road, Deborah's death and Rachel's death, Israel journeys on, the pilgrim still moving toward his father's house at Hebron, so Israel moves on and pitches beyond the tower of Edar).

themes: And Israel journeyed
and spread his tent beyond the tower of Edar
Israel resuming the pilgrim journey after grief
still moving toward his father's house at Hebron

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: when Israel dwelt in that land Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine, and Israel heard it; Gill: this was near Bethlehem, and the matter is recorded briefly; the witnesses note Reuben lying with Bilhah his father's concubine, Israel hearing of it, and the sons of Jacob being twelve — the grievous sin of the firstborn, passed over briefly, that forfeited his birthright (a dark deed staining the firstborn: 'when Israel dwelt in that land, that Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine: and Israel heard it. Now the sons of Jacob were twelve'; the gravity of the sin, that Reuben, the firstborn, 'lay with Bilhah his father's concubine,' Rachel's handmaid, Jacob's secondary wife, a great and shameful incestuous wrong against his own father; the brevity of the record, the matter named and passed over quickly, 'Israel heard it' and no more here said, the Scripture drawing a veil over the shame yet not hiding the sin; the lasting consequence, that for this deed Reuben forfeited the dignity of the firstborn, as Jacob declared on his deathbed (49:3-4, 'unstable as water, thou shalt not excel'); the joining of the count, 'the sons of Jacob were twelve,' turning from the sin to number the twelve patriarchs who follow, so Reuben's sin is briefly told and the twelve sons numbered).

debates.question: What was the consequence of Reuben's sin with Bilhah?

sides.position: He forfeited the dignity and double portion of the firstborn — as Jacob declared on his deathbed (Genesis 49:3-4, 'unstable as water, thou shalt not excel')

themes: Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine: and Israel heard it
Now the sons of Jacob were twelve
the grievous sin of the firstborn, briefly told and passed over
a wrong that forfeited the birthright, as Jacob declared at his death

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the sons of Leah: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn, and Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun; Gill: Leah was Jacob's first wife, whose six sons are reckoned in order according to their birth; the witnesses note the six sons of Leah listed in birth order, Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun — the roll of the patriarchs begun with Leah's sons, the heads of the tribes (the Scripture now numbering the twelve by their mothers, beginning with Leah's: 'The sons of Leah; Reuben, Jacob's firstborn, and Simeon, and Levi, and Judah, and Issachar, and Zebulun'; the order, that Leah's six sons are set first, 'Reuben, Jacob's firstborn,' the eldest though he forfeited the birthright, then 'Simeon, and Levi,' whose anger Jacob would curse, 'and Judah,' from whom the royal and Messianic line would come, 'and Issachar, and Zebulun,' reckoned in the order of their birth; the dignity of this naming, that these are the heads of the tribes of Israel, the foundation of the nation God had just promised; that the roll begins with Leah, the less-loved wife, whose sons were the more in number, so the six sons of Leah head the roll of the twelve patriarchs).

themes: The sons of Leah; Reuben, Jacob's firstborn
and Simeon, and Levi, and Judah, and Issachar, and Zebulun
Leah's six sons heading the roll of the patriarchs
the heads of the tribes, the foundation of the promised nation

schools.summary: Gill: then Rachel's sons, Jacob's next wife, though in right his first and only one, who had two children, Joseph and Benjamin; the witnesses note the two sons of Rachel, Joseph and Benjamin — the naming of Rachel's two sons, the beloved wife's portion in the twelve (the roll continuing with Rachel's sons: 'The sons of Rachel; Joseph, and Benjamin'; that Rachel, the beloved wife, whom Gill calls 'in right his first and only one,' the wife of Jacob's first and truest love, bore but 'two children, Joseph and Benjamin'; the weight of these two names, 'Joseph,' the son so loved, who would be sold into Egypt and rise to save his house, the type of Christ, and 'Benjamin,' the son of her sorrow and Jacob's right hand, just born at the cost of her life; the poignancy, that Rachel, who had so longed for children, had but these two and died bearing the second; that, though fewest in number, Rachel's sons were dearest to Jacob, so Rachel's two sons, Joseph and Benjamin, are named among the twelve).

themes: The sons of Rachel; Joseph, and Benjamin
Rachel's two sons named among the twelve
Joseph the beloved and Benjamin the son of her sorrow
fewest in number, yet dearest to Jacob

schools.summary: Gill: then Bilhah's sons, who was Rachel's handmaid, and these were two, Dan and Naphtali; Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note the significance of the tribal names; the witnesses note the two sons of Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid, Dan and Naphtali — the naming of the handmaid's sons, full sharers in the roll of the twelve (the roll continuing with the sons of the handmaids: 'the sons of Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid; Dan, and Naphtali'; that 'Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid,' given to Jacob by Rachel in her barrenness, bore 'Dan, and Naphtali,' who are numbered here among the twelve sons with the rest; that, though sons of the handmaid, Dan and Naphtali were true sons of Jacob and heads of tribes, full sharers in the inheritance and the covenant; the significance the commentators find in the names, Dan 'judge,' Naphtali 'my wrestling,' each a memorial of Rachel's striving for children through her maid; that God's covenant embraced all the twelve, by whatever mother born, so Bilhah's two sons, Dan and Naphtali, are named among the twelve).

themes: the sons of Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid; Dan, and Naphtali
Bilhah's two sons named among the twelve
true sons of Jacob and heads of tribes
God's covenant embracing all the twelve, by whatever mother born

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: born to him in Padan-aram, i.e., all but Benjamin was born in Canaan; Gill: lastly the sons of Zilpah, Leah's handmaid, which were two, Gad and Asher; the witnesses note the two sons of Zilpah, Gad and Asher, and the summary that these sons were born to Jacob in Padan-aram — the completing of the roll of the twelve, with the note of their birthplace (the roll closed with the handmaid's sons and a summing word: 'the sons of Zilpah, Leah's handmaid; Gad, and Asher: these are the sons of Jacob, which were born to him in Padanaram'; that 'Zilpah, Leah's handmaid,' bore 'Gad, and Asher,' the last two of the twelve, full sharers like the rest; the summary, 'these are the sons of Jacob, which were born to him in Padanaram,' a general statement that the sons were born during Jacob's twenty years in Padan-aram; the small exactness the commentators add, with Clarke, that strictly all were born there except Benjamin, who was born in Canaan, the summary speaking of the most; that the twelve, by their four mothers, now stand complete, the founders of the tribes of Israel, so Zilpah's sons close the roll and the twelve are born to Jacob).

themes: the sons of Zilpah, Leah's handmaid; Gad, and Asher
these are the sons of Jacob, which were born to him in Padanaram
Zilpah's two sons closing the roll of the twelve
the twelve founders of the tribes now complete (all save Benjamin born there)

schools.summary: Gill: no mention being made of his mother, it is very probable she was dead; Isaac being alone, and very old, and the time of his death drawing nigh, he might send for Jacob to come; the witnesses note Jacob coming to Isaac his father at Mamre, the city of Arbah, which is Hebron, where Abraham and Isaac had sojourned — the long journey home completed, the son come at last to his aged father (Jacob reaching the end of his long pilgrimage home: 'Jacob came unto Isaac his father unto Mamre, unto the city of Arbah, which is Hebron, where Abraham and Isaac sojourned'; the homecoming, that Jacob, after twenty years in Padan-aram and the slow journey through Shechem and Bethel, comes at last 'unto Isaac his father,' to the family home 'at Mamre... which is Hebron,' the very place 'where Abraham and Isaac sojourned' as pilgrims; the absence of Rebekah from the record, that no mention is made of Jacob's mother, who had sent him away and longed to see him return but who, it is gathered, had died before he came, so that he found his father alone and very aged; the tenderness of the meeting, the son returned to the father in his last years, so Jacob comes home at last to his aged father at Hebron).

themes: Jacob came unto Isaac his father unto Mamre, unto the city of Arbah, which is Hebron
where Abraham and Isaac sojourned
Jacob's long journey home completed at Hebron
come at last to his aged father, Rebekah gathered to have died

schools.summary: Gill: Isaac lived forty years after he had made his will and blessed his two sons; Jacob was now one hundred and twenty years of age, being born when his father was sixty; the witnesses note the days of Isaac being a hundred and eighty years — the full span of Isaac's long life numbered (the Scripture recording the length of Isaac's days: 'the days of Isaac were an hundred and fourscore years'; the great age, that Isaac lived 'a hundred and fourscore years,' 180, the longest-lived of the three patriarchs; the reckoning the commentators draw, that Isaac lived long after he had thought himself dying and blessed his sons, some forty years more, so that the blessing was given when his death seemed near but was yet far off; that Jacob was now a hundred and twenty, Isaac having been sixty at Jacob's birth; the quiet dignity of the record, numbering the days of the patriarch whose long life now drew to its close, so the full span of Isaac's hundred and eighty years is told).

themes: And the days of Isaac were an hundred and fourscore years
the full span of Isaac's long life numbered
the longest-lived of the three patriarchs
who lived forty years after he blessed his sons thinking himself dying

schools.summary: Gill: Isaac died old and full of days, and his children buried him in the cave of Machpelah, the burying-place of the family; the witnesses note Isaac giving up the ghost, dying, and being gathered to his people, old and full of days, his sons Esau and Jacob burying him — the serene death of the patriarch and the reconciled brothers united at his grave (the chapter closing with Isaac's peaceful end: 'Isaac gave up the ghost, and died, and was gathered unto his people, being old and full of days: and his sons Esau and Jacob buried him'; the peace of his passing, that Isaac 'gave up the ghost, and died,' and 'was gathered unto his people,' a phrase of hope that he joined the company of the faithful dead, Abraham and Sarah before him, 'being old and full of days,' having lived a long and full life, satisfied with days; the touching detail of the burial, 'his sons Esau and Jacob buried him,' the two brothers, once at deadly enmity but now reconciled, standing together at their father's grave, united in the last office of love, as the brothers had been at Abraham's burial; the fitness of this close, the patriarch laid to rest in peace by his reconciled sons, so Isaac dies full of days and is buried by Esau and Jacob together).

themes: Isaac gave up the ghost, and died, and was gathered unto his people, being old and full of days
and his sons Esau and Jacob buried him
the serene death of Isaac, gathered to his people
the reconciled brothers united at their father's grave
